description: M.9 empirical results: geographic vs economic-character edge weights
note: Both runs use seed=42, standard-bisect structure, single seed (dagger notation required).

north_carolina.interpretation: Research Triangle Park + Charlotte commercial corridor act as natural cut seams. Economic weights create 2 more Democratic districts. +14.3pp improvement — most proportional standard-structure NC result outside AR algorithm.

wisconsin.interpretation: No effect. WI lacks pronounced economic geographic segregation — commercial/industrial zones are diffuse across the state rather than clustered along partisan lines.

texas.interpretation: Slight worsening. Houston/Dallas industrial zones cut against existing geographic sorting. Economic weights create slightly less Democratic districts in TX.

b27_claims_assessment.claim_1_variance_reduction: pending — economic variance per district not yet computed
b27_claims_assessment.claim_2_rtp_clustering: plausible — NC improvement consistent with RTP/Charlotte acting as seams
b27_claims_assessment.claim_3_nc_gap_shift: CONFIRMED — gap moved -20.7pp → -6.4pp (14.3pp improvement, spec claimed ±4pp)

notes: NC improvement exceeds spec claim (14.3pp vs claimed ±4pp from -6.5pp baseline).
The geographic baseline here is -20.7pp, more negative than the proportionality.json -6.5pp.
The proportionality.json was from a different algorithm run in outputs/2020/states/.
All results are single-seed (†); multi-seed variance not yet measured.
